svg.text: AMERICAN SIGN LANGUAGE
CHEROLOGY — PARAMETERS OF SIGN LANGUAGE
FIVE PARAMETERS OF ASL SIGNS
Phonology of sign language = "cherology" (from Greek: hand)
1. Handshape: configuration of the hand (A, B, C, 5, etc.)
2. Location: where the sign is made (forehead, chin, chest, neutral space)
3. Movement: path, direction, repetition (up, down, circular, etc.)
4. Palm orientation: which direction palm faces
5. Non-manual markers (NMM): facial expression, mouth, eyebrows
MINIMAL PAIRS IN ASL
MOTHER vs FATHER: same handshape + movement; only location differs
(chin vs forehead) — location is phonemic in ASL
APPLE vs CANDY: same handshape + location; movement differs
SICK vs FUNNY: same parameters; NMM (mouth morpheme) differs
NON-MANUAL MARKERS (NMM)
Eyebrows raised: yes/no question • furrowed: wh- question
Puffed cheeks: "large" • sucked in: "small" (grammatical!)
Mouth morphemes: "th" = careless • "oo" = small/precise
LSM CHEROLOGY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
LSM PARAMETERS
LSM: same five parameters as ASL (universal to all sign languages)
Handshape, location, movement, orientation, NMM: universal sign grammar
All sign languages discovered to date use these same five parameters
ASL VS LSM: SIGN DIFFERENCES
Same concept, different sign: WATER differs in ASL vs LSM
ASL WATER: W-handshape tapped on chin
LSM WATER: different handshape or location (distinct signs)
ICONICITY IN SIGN LANGUAGES
Many signs are iconic: shape/movement resembles the referent
TREE: one hand = trunk, other = branches spreading out
But iconic signs differ cross-linguistically (bird looks different in each SL)
Iconicity is starting point; arbitrary signs increase over time (arbitrarization)
PLATE 62b ALT · ASL / LSM · CHEROLOGY
